<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/hexi.h>
#include <vector>
#include <string>
#include <cstdint>
#include <span>

struct NetworkPacket {
    uint32_t packet_id;
    std::string username;
    std::vector<uint32_t> scores;
    std::string metadata;
};

NetworkPacket deserialize_packet(const std::span<const uint8_t> buffer) {
    hexi::buffer_adaptor adaptor(buffer);
    
    NetworkPacket packet;
    
    // Read packet_id
    packet.packet_id = hexi::read<uint32_t>(adaptor);
    
    // Read username length and then username
    uint32_t username_len = hexi::read<uint32_t>(adaptor);
    packet.username = hexi::read_string_adaptor(adaptor, username_len);
    
    // Read scores count and then scores
    uint32_t scores_count = hexi::read<uint32_t>(adaptor);
    packet.scores = hexi::read_container<std::vector<uint32_t>>(adaptor, scores_count);
    
    // Read metadata
    packet.metadata = hexi::read_null_terminated(adaptor);
    
    // Skip remaining padding
    size_t remaining = buffer.size() - adaptor.position();
    hexi::skip(adaptor, remaining);
    
    return packet;
}

struct PacketInfo {
    uint32_t packet_id;
    std::string_view username;
};

PacketInfo extract_packet_info(const std::span<const uint8_t> buffer) {
    hexi::buffer_adaptor adaptor(buffer);
    
    PacketInfo info;
    
    // Zero-copy packet_id using span
    auto id_span = std::span<const uint8_t>(buffer.data(), 4);
    info.packet_id = *reinterpret_cast<const uint32_t>(id_span.data());
    
    // Read username length and string_view
    uint32_t username_len = hexi::read<uint32_t>(adaptor);
    info.username = hexi::read_string_adaptor(adaptor, username_len);
    
    // Skip scores: read count, then skip the data
    uint32_t scores_count = hexi::read<uint32_t>(adaptor);
    size_t scores_bytes = scores_count * sizeof(uint32_t);
    hexi::skip(adaptor, scores_bytes);
    
    return info;
}
```</answer>

struct MessageHeader {
    uint16_t message_type;
    uint32_t body_length;
    uint32_t footer;
};

MessageHeader read_header_and_skip_body(const std::span<const uint8_t> buffer) {
    hexi::InputStream stream(buffer);
    MessageHeader header;
    
    header.message_type = stream.read<uint16_t>();
    header.body_length = stream.read<uint32_t>();
    stream.skip(header.body_length);
    header.footer = stream.read<uint32_t>();
    
    return header;
}
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/hexi.h>
#include <vector>
#include <cstdint>
#include <span>

struct DataResult {
    uint8_t data_type;
    std::vector<uint32_t> int_items;
    std::vector<float> float_items;
};

DataResult preview_and_read_data(const std::span<const uint8_t> buffer) {
    hexi::buffer_adaptor adaptor(buffer);
    hexi::binary_stream stream(adaptor);
    
    // Peek at the data type without advancing the read pointer
    uint8_t data_type = stream.peek<uint8_t>(0);
    
    // Seek to the position after the data type and read item count
    stream.seek(1);
    uint32_t item_count = stream.read<uint32_t>();
    
    DataResult result;
    result.data_type = data_type;
    
    // Read items based on the data type
    if (data_type == 0) {
        // Integer items
        for (uint32_t i = 0; i < item_count; ++i) {
            result.int_items.push_back(stream.read<uint32_t>());
        }
    } else {
        // Float items
        for (uint32_t i = 0; i < item_count; ++i) {
            result.float_items.push_back(stream.read<float>());
        }
    }
    
    return result;
}
```</answer>

#include <hexi/dynamic_buffer.h>
#include <vector>
#include <cstdint>

template<size_t block_size = 1024>
void write_to_dynamic_buffer(hexi::dynamic_buffer<block_size>& buffer, uint32_t header, const std::vector<uint8_t>& data) {
    buffer.write(&header, sizeof(header));
    buffer.write(data.data(), data.size());
}

template<size_t block_size = 1024>
struct BufferData {
    uint32_t header;
    std::vector<uint8_t> data;
};

BufferData<> read_from_dynamic_buffer(hexi::dynamic_buffer<1024>& buffer, size_t data_length) {
    BufferData<> result;
    uint32_t header;
    buffer.read(&header, sizeof(header));
    
    std::vector<uint8_t> data(data_length);
    buffer.read(data.data(), data_length);
    
    result.header = header;
    result.data = std::move(data);
    return result;
}
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/allocators/block_allocator.h>
#include <cstdint>
#include <iostream>

template<size_t element_count = 10>
void demonstrate_block_allocator_behavior() {
    // Create a block_allocator instance with the specified number of blocks
    hexi::block_allocator<element_count> allocator;
    
    std::cout << "=== Block Allocator Free List Demonstration ===\n";
    std::cout << "Initial state - Freshly created allocator:\n";
    std::cout << "All blocks are available in the free list.\n\n";
    
    // First allocation: This should pop a block from the free list
    void* block1 = allocator.allocate();
    std::cout << "1. First allocation:\n";
    std::cout << "   - A block was allocated from the free list.\n";
    std::cout << "   - Address: " << block1 << "\n";
    std::cout << "   - The free list now has " << (element_count - 1) 
              << " blocks remaining (internally popped by push/pop)\n\n";
    
    // Second allocation: This should pop another block
    void* block2 = allocator.allocate();
    std::cout << "2. Second allocation:\n";
    std::cout << "   - Another block was allocated.\n";
    std::cout << "   - Address: " << block2 << "\n";
    std::cout << "   - The free list now has " << (element_count - 2) 
              << " blocks remaining\n\n";
    
    // Deallocate the first block: This should push it back to the free list
    allocator.deallocate(block1);
    std::cout << "3. Deallocating first block:\n";
    std::cout << "   - The block at address " << block1 << " was returned to the free list.\n";
    std::cout << "   - Now, the free list has " << (element_count - 1) 
              << " blocks available (internally pushed back)\n\n";
    
    // Third allocation: This should reuse the previously deallocated block
    void* block3 = allocator.allocate();
    std::cout << "4. Third allocation:\n";
    std::cout << "   - A block was allocated from the free list.\n";
    std::cout << "   - Address: " << block3 << "\n";
    
    // Check if the third block reused the first block's address
    if (block3 == block1) {
        std::cout << "   - **CRITICAL OBSERVATION**: The address matches!\n";
        std::cout << "   - The block was REUSED from the free list (push/pop in action)\n";
    } else {
        std::cout << "   - The block was allocated from a new chunk (free list exhausted)\n";
    }
    std::cout << "   - The free list now has " << (element_count - 2) 
              << " blocks remaining\n\n";
    
    // Final cleanup
    allocator.deallocate(block2);
    allocator.deallocate(block3);
    std::cout << "5. All blocks deallocated - Free list restored to full capacity (" 
              << element_count << " blocks)\n";
    std::cout << "=== Demonstration Complete ===\n";
}

// Example usage:
// int main() {
//     demonstrate_block_allocator_behavior<5>(); // Use 5 blocks for demonstration
//     return 0;
// }
```

**Key Points Demonstrated:**
1. **Push/Pop Behavior**: When a block is deallocated (`pop`), it's returned to the free list. When allocated again (`push`), it's reused.
2. **Address Reuse**: The critical observation shows that deallocated blocks are available for future allocations, proving the free list management.
3. **Capacity Management**: The allocator efficiently manages a fixed number of blocks, reusing them instead of constantly allocating/deallocating memory.

This solution leverages the public `allocate`/`deallocate` interface to indirectly demonstrate the internal `push`/`pop` operations of the free list.</answer>

#include <hexi/endian.h>
#include <cstdint>

template<typename T>
T convert_to_big_endian(T value) {
    return hexi::endian::native_to_big(value);
}

template<typename T>
T convert_from_big_endian(T value) {
    return hexi::endian::big_to_native(value);
}

template<typename T>
T convert_to_little_endian(T value) {
    return hexi::endian::native_to_little(value);
}

template<typename T>
T convert_from_little_endian(T value) {
    return hexi::endian::little_to_native(value);
}
```</answer>

    return status;

template<size_t block_size = 1024>
void reset_buffer(hexi::detail::intrusive_storage<block_size>& storage) {
    storage.reset();
}

<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/file_buffer.h>
#include <vector>
#include <cstdint>
#include <filesystem>

std::vector<uint8_t> read_file_data(const std::filesystem::path& file_path, size_t bytes_to_read) {
    hexi::file_buffer file(file_path, hexi::file_buffer::read);
    std::vector<uint8_t> buffer(bytes_to_read);
    size_t bytes_read = file.read(buffer.data(), bytes_to_read);
    buffer.resize(bytes_read);
    return buffer;
}

std::vector<uint8_t> preview_file_data(const std::filesystem::path& file_path, size_t bytes_to_preview) {
    hexi::file_buffer file(file_path, hexi::file_buffer::read);
    auto current_pos = file.tell();
    std::vector<uint8_t> buffer(bytes_to_preview);
    size_t bytes_read = file.read(buffer.data(), bytes_to_preview);
    buffer.resize(bytes_read);
    file.seek(current_pos); // Restore original position
    return buffer;
}

void write_file_data(const std::filesystem::path& file_path, const std::vector<uint8_t>& data) {
    hexi::file_buffer file(file_path, hexi::file_buffer::write);
    file.write(data.data(), data.size());
}

size_t get_file_size(const std::filesystem::path& file_path) {
    hexi::file_buffer file(file_path, hexi::file_buffer::read);
    auto size = file.seek(0, hexi::file_buffer::end); // Seek to end to get size
    file.seek(0, hexi::file_buffer::beg); // Seek back to beginning
    return size;
}
```</answer>

    fb.copy(header.data(), header_size);

}

template<size_t buffer_size = 1024>
void write_data_to_buffer(hexi::static_buffer<std::byte, buffer_size>& buffer, const std::vector<uint8_t>& data) {

#include <hexi/static_buffer.h>

    size_t capacity;
    size_t current_size;
    size_t free_space;
};

#include <hexi/pmc/binary_stream_writer.h>
#include <hexi/pmc/binary_stream_reader.h>
#include <hexi/pmc/buffer_adaptor.h>
#include <hexi/pmc/stream_adaptors.h>
#include <vector>
#include <cstdint>
#include <string>

struct UserData {
    uint32_t user_id;
    std::string name;
    
    void serialise(auto& stream) {
        stream << user_id << name;
    }
};

void serialize_object(hexi::pmc::binary_stream_writer& writer, const UserData& data) {
    hexi::pmc::buffer_adaptor adaptor = writer.buffer_adaptor();
    data.serialise(adaptor);
}

void deserialize_object(hexi::pmc::binary_stream_reader& reader, UserData& data) {
    hexi::pmc::buffer_adaptor adaptor = reader.buffer_adaptor();
    data.serialise(adaptor);
}
```

**Explanation:**
- The `serialize_object` function creates a `buffer_adaptor` from the `binary_stream_writer` and passes it to the `UserData` object's `serialise` method, which writes the data to the stream.
- The `deserialize_object` function similarly creates a `buffer_adaptor` from the `binary_stream_reader` and uses the same `serialise` method to read data from the stream into the `UserData` object.
- This approach leverages the dual-purpose `serialise` method for both operations, ensuring consistency and reducing code duplication.</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/pmc/buffer_adaptor.h>
#include <vector>
#include <cstdint>

void write_data_to_buffer(hexi::pmc::buffer_adaptor<std::vector<std::byte>>& buffer, uint32_t value, const void* data, std::size_t length) {
    // Write the integer value
    buffer.write(reinterpret_cast<const std::byte*>(&value), sizeof(value));
    // Write the byte array
    buffer.write(static_cast<const std::byte*>(data), length);
}

void read_data_from_buffer(hexi::pmc::buffer_adaptor<std::vector<std::byte>>& buffer, uint32_t& value, void* data, std::size_t length) {
    // Read the integer value
    buffer.read(reinterpret_cast<std::byte*>(&value), sizeof(value));
    // Read the byte array
    buffer.read(static_cast<std::byte*>(data), length);
}

struct BufferInfo {
    std::size_t size;
    std::size_t free_space;
};

BufferInfo get_buffer_info(hexi::pmc::buffer_adaptor<std::vector<std::byte>>& buffer) {
    return {buffer.size(), buffer.free()};
}
```</answer>

#include <hexi/pmc/buffer_adaptor.h>

using namespace hexi::detail;

template<typename T>
std::size_t encode_varint(hexi::pmc::binary_stream_writer& writer, T value) {
    auto uvalue = static_cast<uint64_t>(value);
    std::size_t bytes_written = 0;
    
    while (uvalue > 0x7F) {
        const uint8_t byte = (uvalue & 0x7F) | 0x80;
        writer.write_byte(byte);
        uvalue >>= 7;
        bytes_written++;
    }
    
    const uint8_t byte = uvalue & 0x7F;
    writer.write_byte(byte);
    return ++bytes_written;
}

template<typename T>
T decode_varint(hexi::pmc::binary_stream_reader& reader) {
    uint64_t result = 0;
    int shift = 0;
    
    while (true) {
        uint8_t byte = reader.read_byte();
        result |= static_cast<uint64_t>(byte & 0x7F) << shift;
        shift += 7;
        
        if ((byte & 0x80) == 0) {
            break;
        }
    }
    
    return static_cast<T>(result);
}

}

<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/hexi.h>
#include <string>
#include <string_view>

void write_string_prefixed_varint(hexi::binary_stream& stream, const std::string& str) {
    stream << hexi::prefixed_varint(str);
}

std::string read_string_prefixed_varint(hexi::binary_stream& stream) {
    std::string_view sv;
    stream >> hexi::prefixed_varint(sv);
    return std::string(sv);
}
```</answer>

Yes, this is the same as before. The lambda takes the value and the index sequence, then expands the parameter pack to create an array of Size elements, each set to 'val'. The (void)I is to avoid unused variable warnings.

This approach is constexpr and works at compile time.

So the final code would look like this.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/shared.h>
#include <array>
#include <cstdint>
#include <type_traits>

using namespace hexi::detail;

template<std::size_t Size>
constexpr auto create_filled_array(std::uint8_t value) {
    return []<std::size_t... I>(std::uint8_t val, std::index_sequence<I...>) constexpr {
        return std::array<std::uint8_t, sizeof...(I)>{((void)I, val)...};
    }(value, std::make_index_sequence<Size>{});
}

bool verify_filled_array(const auto& arr, std::uint8_t expected_value) {
    for (const auto& element : arr) {
        if (element != expected_value) {
            return false;
        }
    }
    return true;
}
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/hexi.h>
#include <string>
#include <cstdint>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>

struct PaddedData {
    uint16_t header;
    std::string data;
    uint32_t footer;
};

std::vector<uint8_t> serialize_with_padding(const PaddedData& packet) {
    std::vector<uint8_t> buffer;
    
    // Reserve space for efficiency
    buffer.reserve(sizeof(packet.header) + sizeof(uint16_t) + packet.data.size() + 16 + sizeof(packet.footer));
    
    // Write header
    const uint8_t* header_bytes = reinterpret_cast<const uint8_t*>(&packet.header);
    buffer.insert(buffer.end(), header_bytes, header_bytes + sizeof(packet.header));
    
    // Write data length (uint16_t)
    uint16_t data_len = static_cast<uint16_t>(packet.data.size());
    const uint8_t* len_bytes = reinterpret_cast<const uint8_t*>(&data_len);
    buffer.insert(buffer.end(), len_bytes, len_bytes + sizeof(data_len));
    
    // Write data string
    buffer.insert(buffer.end(), packet.data.begin(), packet.data.end());
    
    // Write 16 bytes of padding (0x00) using fill<16>(0x00)
    auto padding = hexi::fill<16>(0x00);
    buffer.insert(buffer.end(), padding.begin(), padding.end());
    
    // Write footer
    const uint8_t* footer_bytes = reinterpret_cast<const uint8_t*>(&packet.footer);
    buffer.insert(buffer.end(), footer_bytes, footer_bytes + sizeof(packet.footer));
    
    return buffer;
}
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/allocators/block_allocator.h>
#include <vector>
#include <memory>

struct PoolObject {
    int id;
    double value;
    
    PoolObject() : id(0), value(0.0) {}
    PoolObject(int i, double v) : id(i), value(v) {}
};

std::vector<PoolObject*> create_object_pool(hexi::block_allocator<PoolObject, 10>& allocator) {
    std::vector<PoolObject*> objects;
    
    // Allocate and construct three objects with different values
    void* mem1 = allocator.allocate(1);
    new(mem1) PoolObject(1, 1.1);
    objects.push_back(static_cast<PoolObject*>(mem1));
    
    void* mem2 = allocator.allocate(1);
    new(mem2) PoolObject(2, 2.2);
    objects.push_back(static_cast<PoolObject*>(mem2));
    
    void* mem3 = allocator.allocate(1);
    new(mem3) PoolObject(3, 3.3);
    objects.push_back(static_cast<PoolObject*>(mem3));
    
    return objects;
}
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/pmc/buffer_base.h>
#include <vector>
#include <cstddef>

class CustomBuffer : public hexi::pmc::buffer_base {
    std::vector<std::byte> data_;
    
public:
    std::size_t size() const override {
        return data_.size();
    }
    
    bool empty() const override {
        return data_.empty();
    }
    
    // Helper method to add data (for testing)
    void add_data(const void* source, std::size_t length) {
        const std::byte* bytes = static_cast<const std::byte*>(source);
        data_.insert(data_.end(), bytes, bytes + length);
    }
    
    // Helper method to clear data (for testing)
    void clear() {
        data_.clear();
    }
};
```</answer>

output.reasoning:
<think>Okay, I need to implement these two functions using the Hexi library's template methods. Let me start by understanding what the problem requires.

First, the `write_typed_data` function takes a TypedData object and writes its fields (id, value, ratio) into a vector of bytes. The write operations should use the template `write(auto& source)` method. So for each field in TypedData, I need to call write on each member.

Then, the `read_typed_data` function reads from a byte vector and returns a TypedData object. Here, I need to use the template `read(T* destination)` method for each field. That means I'll have to read each value in the correct order and assign them to the corresponding members of the TypedData struct.

Wait, but how does the Hexi library work here? The user mentioned using buffer_read_adaptor and buffer_write_adaptor. Oh right, probably these adaptors are used to handle the actual reading and writing to/from the buffer. So maybe the functions need to create an instance of these adaptors with the buffer, then use their methods.

Let me think about the steps for write_typed_data:

1. Create a buffer_write_adaptor with the output vector.
2. Write each field of the TypedData using the adaptor's write method.
3. Return the buffer after writing all data.

For read_typed_data:

1. Create a buffer_read_adaptor with the input vector.
2. Read each field in the same order they were written, assigning to the TypedData members.
3. Return the populated TypedData object.

But wait, the functions are supposed to return the vector for writing and take the vector as input for reading. So the adaptors need to be initialized with the buffer passed to the functions.

So for write_typed_data:

- Initialize a buffer_write_adaptor with the output vector.
- Then call write on each field: adaptor.write(data.id), adaptor.write(data.value), adaptor.write(data.ratio).

For read_typed_data:

- Initialize a buffer_read_adaptor with the input buffer.
- Read each field in order: adaptor.read(&data.id), adaptor.read(&data.value), adaptor.read(&data.ratio).
- Then return data.

But I need to make sure that the buffer_read_adaptor and buffer_write_adaptor are properly included. The user provided the includes, so that's taken care of.

Now, putting this into code:

The write function:

std::vector<uint8_t> write_typed_data(const TypedData& data) {
    std::vector<uint8_t> buffer;
    hexi::pmc::buffer_write_adaptor adaptor(buffer);
    adaptor.write(data.id);
    adaptor.write(data.value);
    adaptor.write(data.ratio);
    return buffer;
}

The read function:

TypedData read_typed_data(const std::vector<uint8_t>& buffer) {
    hexi::pmc::buffer_read_adaptor adaptor(buffer);
    TypedData data;
    adaptor.read(&data.id);
    adaptor.read(&data.value);
    adaptor.read(&data.ratio);
    return data;
}

Wait, but what if the buffer is empty or doesn't have enough data? The problem statement probably assumes that the buffer has the correct amount of data, so we don't need error handling here.

Also, the order of reading must match the order of writing. Since we wrote id first, then value, then ratio, we read them in the same order.

Yes, that makes sense. So the code should work as expected.
output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/pmc/buffer_read_adaptor.h>
#include <hexi/pmc/buffer_write_adaptor.h>
#include <vector>
#include <cstdint>

struct TypedData {
    uint32_t id;
    int16_t value;
    double ratio;
};

std::vector<uint8_t> write_typed_data(const TypedData& data) {
    std::vector<uint8_t> buffer;
    hexi::pmc::buffer_write_adaptor adaptor(buffer);
    adaptor.write(data.id);
    adaptor.write(data.value);
    adaptor.write(data.ratio);
    return buffer;
}

TypedData read_typed_data(const std::vector<uint8_t>& buffer) {
    hexi::pmc::buffer_read_adaptor adaptor(buffer);
    TypedData data;
    adaptor.read(&data.id);
    adaptor.read(&data.value);
    adaptor.read(&data.ratio);
    return data;
}
```</answer>

output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/pmc/buffer_read_adaptor.h>
#include <hexi/pmc/buffer_write_adaptor.h>
#include <vector>
#include <cstdint>
#include <algorithm>

std::size_t write_multiple_chunks(const std::vector<std::vector<uint8_t>>& chunks) {
    hexi::pmc::buffer_write_adaptor adaptor;
    std::size_t total_size = 0;
    for (const auto& chunk : chunks) {
        std::size_t free_space = adaptor.free();
        std::size_t write_size = std::min(chunk.size(), free_space);
        adaptor.write(chunk.data(), write_size);
        total_size += write_size;
    }
    return total_size;
}

std::vector<uint8_t> read_partial_data(const std::vector<uint8_t>& buffer, std::size_t chunk_size) {
    hexi::pmc::buffer_read_adaptor adaptor(buffer);
    std::vector<uint8_t> result;
    while (adaptor.size() > 0) {
        std::size_t read_size = std::min(chunk_size, adaptor.size());
        auto data = adaptor.read(read_size);
        result.insert(result.end(), data.begin(), data.end());
    }
    return result;
}
```</answer>

<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/pmc/buffer_read_adaptor.h>
#include <vector>
#include <cstdint>
#include <cstring>

struct Header {
    uint32_t magic;
    uint16_t version;
};

Header peek_header_without_reading(const std::vector<uint8_t>& buffer) {
    hexi::buffer_read_adaptor adaptor(buffer);
    
    // Save current read pointer to verify it doesn't change
    auto current_ptr = adaptor.read_ptr();
    
    // Peek at the header data (6 bytes) without consuming it
    auto header_data = adaptor.copy(6);
    
    // Verify the read pointer remains unchanged
    auto new_ptr = adaptor.read_ptr();
    // current_ptr should equal new_ptr after copy()
    
    // Construct Header from the copied data
    Header header;
    // Assuming little-endian byte order; adjust if necessary for your system
    std::memcpy(&header.magic, header_data.data(), sizeof(header.magic));
    std::memcpy(&header.version, header_data.data() + 4, sizeof(header.version));
    
    return header;
}

std::vector<uint8_t> peek_then_conditionally_read(const std::vector<uint8_t>& buffer) {
    hexi::buffer_read_adaptor adaptor(buffer);
    
    // Peek at the first byte
    auto first_byte = adaptor.copy(1);
    
    // Condition: if the first byte is non-zero, read the entire buffer
    if (first_byte[0] != 0) {
        // Read the entire buffer from the current position
        return adaptor.read(adaptor.size());
    } else {
        // Return empty vector if the condition is not met
        return {};
    }
}
```</answer>
